_meta.title: Educational Resources
_meta.note: Educational Resources for the WLAN Pros Toolbox: independent-author and community materials only. Per Keith (2026-06-04) the megavendor/product documentation entries (Apple, Cisco, Aruba/HPE, and the Hamina product docs) are EXCLUDED; the dataset keeps community tools/utilities, independent blogs and experts, podcasts, conference and talk archives, YouTube channels, and training/certification paths. Independent-author entries are included now with permission chased afterward (approval='pending_outreach'); any owner who declines gets removed. Descriptions are Keith's voice (GL-004, SOP-020 PASS). The full vendor-doc set is retained in the source dataset and git history if ever wanted back. Also still held: Wi-Fi For Beginners (site not confirmed live) and frames.bitbucketlabs.com (pending Keith's 1-line description). Curated 2026-06-04.

resources.title: Wi-Fi Clients List — Device Capabilities Database
resources.summary: Look up what a client device can actually do on Wi-Fi before you design around it.
resources.description: Before you assume a client can use that 160 MHz channel or a third spatial stream, check what it actually supports. Mike Albano's Wi-Fi Clients List is a community-contributed database of client device capabilities: spatial streams, supported bands, and channel support, device by device. When you are sizing capacity or chasing down why one device performs differently from the rest, this is where you confirm what the client is really capable of instead of guessing. Browsing is free. You only need an account if you want to contribute new devices and grow the database.

resources.title: Wireless PCAP I/O Graph Analyzer
resources.summary: Drop in an 802.11 capture and see the airtime over time without the manual Wireshark workflow.
resources.description: Mohammad Ali's PCAP I/O Graph Analyzer, from his Art of RF site, takes an 802.11 capture, lets you apply Wireshark-style display filters, and draws an I/O graph of the wireless traffic over time. It is the fast way to see traffic and airtime patterns and spot anomalies, then hand the picture to someone else, without building the I/O graph by hand every time. It works on 802.11 frames only, so a non-Wi-Fi capture will not load. The author calls it a work in progress and notes it is not meant to replace professional analysis tools and may not always display data accurately. Treat it as a quick visual first look, then confirm anything important in your full toolset.

resources.title: WLAN Pi
resources.summary: The open-source, pocketable Wi-Fi analysis platform for on-site work.
resources.description: WLAN Pi is the open-source Wi-Fi analysis platform built on small single-board hardware, maintained by a community of wireless engineers. It scans, tests, monitors, and troubleshoots wired and wireless networks, which gives you a real diagnostic tool you can carry in a pocket to the job site. The WLAN Pi OS is free and open source. The project funds itself in part by selling hardware, so the software costs nothing and you buy a device only if you want one. If you work in the field, this belongs in your bag.

resources.title: WLAN Pi Cookbook
resources.summary: The official community how-to guide for actually operating a WLAN Pi.
resources.description: The Cookbook is the practical companion to the WLAN Pi project: the official community documentation for setting up and operating the device. It covers getting started, connecting from a phone or a laptop, USB modes, power management, and the access methods you need to know to put the thing to work. If WLAN Pi is the tool, this is the manual that gets you from unboxed to useful. Free to read, no login required.

resources.title: Wavelify — Network Planning Platform
resources.summary: A web-based Wi-Fi design platform with a companion Android survey app.
resources.description: Wavelify is a web-based network planning platform for Wi-Fi and broader low-voltage design. You import a floor plan, place Access Points, and model coverage in 2D and 3D across 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz, with wall and material attenuation and AI-assisted automatic AP placement, then export PDF design reports. A companion Android survey app turns a phone into a capture tool that syncs measurements back into the design project. It is a newer entrant, so give it a look on its own merits. You can get in and try it for free, with the full design capability behind paid tiers.

resources.title: WLPC Conference Talks (WLAN Pros on YouTube)
resources.summary: Over 1,000 recorded WLPC conference talks, free on YouTube, the largest vendor-neutral Wi-Fi talk library anywhere.
resources.description:
If you want to learn Wi-Fi from the people actually deploying it, this is where you start. The Wireless LAN Professionals YouTube channel holds more than 1,000 recorded WLPC conference presentations going back to 2014, in English and Spanish. Real engineers, real deployments, no vendor pitch. The talks run the full range, from packet analysis and survey method to security, design, and the standards work itself.

This is the WLPC conference archive, and it is free. Most sessions are short and focused, so you can watch one over lunch and walk away with something you can use the same afternoon. Start with a topic you are weak on and work outward from there.

resources.title: WLPC Video Library (wlanprofessionals.com)
resources.summary: The WLAN Pros landing page for the WLPC video archive, the front door to the full talk library.
resources.description:
This is the WLAN Pros video library page, the front door to the WLPC talk archive. It surfaces the latest conference videos and points you to the complete collection on the Wireless LAN Professionals YouTube channel.

It is a gateway, not a separate searchable index, so think of it as the place to land when you want to browse what is new rather than hunt for one specific talk. Free, no login. If you would rather go straight to everything, the YouTube channel itself is the deeper well.

resources.title: Wi-Fi Design Day On-Demand (Open Reality)
resources.summary: Free on-demand recordings and handouts from more than 70 Wi-Fi Design Day sessions on survey and design.
resources.description:
Wi-Fi Design Day is a UK Wi-Fi event run by Open Reality and Ekahau, and this on-demand page opens up the back catalog. You get free access to recordings and session handouts from more than 70 talks covering surveying, design, security, monitoring, and troubleshooting.

The handouts are the part people overlook. A recording teaches you the thinking, but the slide deck and notes are what you keep on your drive for the next project. Registration is required to get in, and the content itself costs nothing. Strong material if survey and design are where you want to level up.

resources.title: Wi-Fi Design Day
resources.summary: The Wi-Fi Design Day event homepage, where the next event gets announced (Open Reality and Ekahau).
resources.description:
The Wi-Fi Design Day event homepage, run by Open Reality and Ekahau. This is where the next event date and agenda show up when one is scheduled.

If you want the talks rather than the event, go to the on-demand recordings page instead. Keep this one bookmarked for when you want to know whether a live Design Day is coming up. Free.

resources.title: CWNP on YouTube (CWNPTV)
resources.summary: CWNP's free webinar and explainer channel, vendor-neutral Wi-Fi education from the certification body.
resources.description:
The CWNP YouTube channel is the video arm of the vendor-neutral certification program, with free webinars and explainers from CWNP and Tom Carpenter. Expect Wi-Fi Visualized walkthroughs, lab builds, and standards explainers, the kind of material that maps straight onto the CWNA and CWAP body of knowledge.

CWNP sells certifications and training, and the videos are free to watch. If you are working toward a CWNP credential, this channel is teaching the same concepts the exams test, in a format you can replay until it sticks.

resources.title: Ekahau on YouTube
resources.summary: Ekahau's Wi-Fi design, survey, and troubleshooting tutorials, free to watch from the survey-tool maker.
resources.description:
The Ekahau YouTube channel publishes Wi-Fi design, survey, and troubleshooting tutorials alongside product webinars. Even where the demos use Ekahau tools, the underlying method, how to plan a survey, read coverage, and validate a design, carries over to whatever survey tool you run.

This is a vendor channel, free to watch, and Ekahau sells design tools. Watch it for the survey thinking, and take the workflow lessons with you regardless of what is in your own bag.

resources.title: Hamina Wireless on YouTube
resources.summary: Hamina's Wi-Fi design-method webinars, including Lunch and Learn basics and guest sessions on 6 GHz.
resources.description:
The Hamina Wireless YouTube channel runs webinars and design-method content, with a Lunch and Learn series on Wi-Fi basics and guest sessions such as Wi-Fi Visualized with Tom Carpenter and 6 GHz with Kevin Robinson. The guest lineup is the draw, since you get named experts walking through real design topics.

This is a vendor channel, free to watch, and Hamina sells planning software. The Lunch and Learn series is a good entry point if you are still building your fundamentals, and the guest sessions reward intermediate engineers who want a deeper cut.

resources.title: Clear To Send on YouTube
resources.summary: Video versions of the Clear To Send podcast plus Wi-Fi engineering explainers, free and independent.
resources.description:
The Clear To Send YouTube channel carries the video edition of the long-running podcast along with standalone Wi-Fi engineering explainers. It is run by Rowell Dionicio and Francois Verges, two working engineers, with no vendor steering the content.

If you prefer to watch a diagram get drawn rather than just hear it described, the video versions are worth it over the audio-only feed. Free. Aimed at engineers who already have the basics and want to go a layer deeper.

resources.title: Heavy Wireless (Packet Pushers)
resources.summary: Keith Parsons' Packet Pushers podcast on Wi-Fi, IoT, and wireless security, with working engineers as guests.
resources.description:
Heavy Wireless is the Wi-Fi show on the Packet Pushers network, hosted by Keith Parsons, CWNE #3. New episodes land every other Tuesday and run deep on technical Wi-Fi, IoT, and wireless security, with engineers and vendors who actually build and deploy the technology.

The format is a working conversation, not a product pitch. Guests get pushed on how things really behave in the field, which is where the value is. Free wherever you get podcasts. Good for any engineer who wants the current thinking on where Wi-Fi is headed and why.

resources.title: Clear To Send (Podcast)
resources.summary: The longest-running Wi-Fi engineering podcast, going since 2015, on design, troubleshooting, and tools.
resources.description:
Clear To Send is the wireless network engineering podcast, hosted by Rowell Dionicio and Francois Verges and running since August 2015. That is a decade of back catalog on design, troubleshooting, tools, and interviews with people across the industry, which makes it one of the deepest free libraries of Wi-Fi audio you will find.

The long run is the point. When you hit a topic you are weak on, there is a good chance an episode already covers it. Listening is free, and the site also sells courses. Aimed at engineers who already know the fundamentals and want to keep current.

resources.title: The Wi-Fi of Things
resources.summary: Ferney Munoz's bilingual English and Spanish Wi-Fi education hub, one of the few covering Wi-Fi in Spanish.
resources.description:
The Wi-Fi of Things, run by Ferney Munoz, is a Wi-Fi education hub published in both English and Spanish, with sessions, industry news, and tool reviews. The Spanish-language coverage is what sets it apart, since very little serious Wi-Fi training exists in Spanish at all.

If you work with Spanish-speaking teams, or you want a second voice teaching the same concepts in a second language, this is a rare resource. Free, independent. Useful across all levels.

resources.title: Wi-Fi Ninjas
resources.summary: Mac and Matt's Wi-Fi podcast and blog on wireless networking, protocol analysis, and industry events.
resources.description:
Wi-Fi Ninjas is a podcast and blog run by Mac and Matt, covering wireless networking, protocol analysis, and industry events, with some content on how they build and share their work. It is an independent show, no vendor behind it.

The protocol-analysis episodes are the strongest material here, so reach for those if packet work is where you want to grow. Free. Aimed at engineers comfortable with the fundamentals.

resources.title: RUCKCast (RUCKUS)
resources.summary: Jim Palmer's informal Wi-Fi and networking podcast from RUCKUS Networks, with guests and product features.
resources.description:
RUCKCast is an informal Wi-Fi and networking podcast hosted by Jim Palmer, CWNE #304, at RUCKUS Networks. It mixes guest conversations with RUCKUS product features, in a loose, easy-listening format.

This is a vendor podcast, free to listen. The guest episodes carry the most general value, since the conversations range across Wi-Fi topics beyond any one product. Worth a spot in the rotation if you run RUCKUS gear or want a lighter listen alongside the deeper technical shows.

resources.title: mrn-cciew
resources.summary: Rasika Nayanajith's deep technical Wi-Fi and packet-analysis blog, one of the best free cert-prep libraries online.
resources.description:
mrn-cciew, by Rasika Nayanajith (CWNE #153, CCIE #22989), is a long-running, deeply technical blog of Wi-Fi and packet-analysis write-ups. It is one of the most active personal blogs in the field, and the cert-prep coverage for CWNP and CCIE is some of the best free material you will find anywhere.

The write-ups go frame by frame, which is exactly what you want when you are studying for an analysis exam or trying to truly understand what a capture is telling you. Free, independent. This one is for engineers chasing the advanced certifications.

resources.title: wirednot
resources.summary: Lee Badman's independent opinion and practitioner commentary on Wi-Fi, the standards bodies, and industry dysfunction.
resources.description:
wirednot, by Lee Badman, is an active independent blog of opinion and practitioner commentary. Badman writes about Wi-Fi from the operator's chair, and he is not shy about calling out where the IEEE working groups, the Wi-Fi Alliance, and the vendors get it wrong.

This is the blog to read when you want a working engineer's honest take rather than a vendor's press release. Free, independent. Best for engineers who want perspective and a check against industry hype, not step-by-step how-tos.

resources.title: Art of RF
resources.summary: Mohammad Ali's practical Wi-Fi and RF blog: validation tooling, packet capture, 802.1X, and platform how-tos.
resources.description:
Art of RF, by Mohammad Ali, is an active blog of hands-on Wi-Fi and RF content, with posts going back to 2012. The focus is practical: validation tooling, packet capture, 802.1X, and how-to walkthroughs for platforms like Mist and Nile.

This is a do-the-work blog, the kind you open when you have a specific job in front of you and want to see how someone else solved it. Mohammad Ali also builds the Wireless PCAP I/O Graph tool, so the analysis posts come from someone who writes his own tooling. Free, independent. Best for intermediate engineers doing real deployment and capture work.

resources.title: The Packetologist
resources.summary: Keith Miller's Wi-Fi troubleshooting deep dives and network automation blog, Python plus Mist and Meraki.
resources.description:
The Packetologist, by Keith Miller, is a blog of Wi-Fi troubleshooting deep dives and network automation, with posts dating back to 2013. The automation angle is the draw, since Miller pairs Wi-Fi work with Python and platform APIs like Mist and Meraki, which is where a lot of the field is heading.

The cadence is on the slower side, so treat it as a reference archive rather than a daily read. When a troubleshooting post lands, it goes deep. Free, independent. Best for advanced engineers who want to bring automation into their wireless work.

resources.title: SemFio Networks Blog
resources.summary: SemFio Networks' practitioner how-tos on WLAN Pi, Python, and regulatory changes, from Francois Verges' consultancy.
resources.description:
The SemFio Networks blog is the writing arm of Francois Verges' consultancy, and it runs to practical how-tos: WLAN Pi material, Python for wireless engineers, and regulatory updates such as changes to Canadian 5 GHz rules.

The regulatory coverage is a useful niche, since spectrum rules shift and most blogs never touch them. The content is free to read, and the consultancy sells services. Best for intermediate engineers who want hands-on technique and want to stay current on the rules that govern what they can actually transmit.

resources.title: Intuitibits Blog
resources.summary: Adrian Granados' Wi-Fi analysis, security, and packet-capture blog, from the maker of WiFi Explorer and Airtool.
resources.description:
The Intuitibits blog, by Adrian Granados, publishes Wi-Fi analysis, security, and packet-capture tutorials, and it stays active. Granados makes WiFi Explorer and Airtool, two macOS tools many of us run every day, so when he writes about a topic like WPA3 or RSN Override, it comes from someone who builds the analysis tooling itself.

The security write-ups are the standouts, since they unpack new protocol behavior the moment it matters. Free to read, and Intuitibits sells tools. Best for advanced engineers doing analysis and security work on Apple gear.

resources.title: Hamina Blog
resources.summary: Hamina Wireless' Wi-Fi design and survey-method blog, with product notes and conference recaps.
resources.description:
The Hamina blog covers Wi-Fi design and survey method, with product notes and conference recaps such as WLPC Phoenix 2026. Even where the examples lean on Hamina's planner, the design and survey thinking carries over to any toolset.

This is a vendor blog (Hamina makes a cloud design tool); the content is free and Hamina sells software. The conference recaps are a fast way to catch what you missed if you could not be in the room. Useful across levels for design and planning work.

resources.title: MackenzieWiFi
resources.summary: Peter Mackenzie's analysis-focused blog and career reflections, from a CWAP study guide co-author.
resources.description:
MackenzieWiFi is the blog of Peter Mackenzie (CWNE #33, CWISE #2), who co-authored the CWAP study guide. The writing leans toward protocol analysis with cert and career reflections from someone who has spent years teaching this material to others.

The analysis pedigree is the reason to read it. When a CWAP-level author writes about captures, it is worth your time. The cadence is on the slower side, so treat it as a reference collection rather than a frequent read. Free, independent. Best for advanced engineers working toward analysis credentials.

resources.title: BadFi (Archive)
resources.summary: Eddie Forero's respected Wi-Fi archive on surveys, captures, 6 GHz, and standards, evergreen and worth reading.
resources.description:
BadFi, by Eddie Forero, is a well-regarded Wi-Fi blog covering surveys, packet captures, 6 GHz, and standards. The last post landed in March 2023, so treat this as an archive rather than an active feed. Eddie himself is still active in the community elsewhere.

The RF physics and standards never went out of date, which is why this one is worth keeping on your list even though it is dormant. Read it as a reference collection. Free, independent. Best for intermediate engineers building their survey and capture fundamentals.

resources.title: wifinigel (Archive)
resources.summary: Nigel Bowden's Wi-Fi scripting and WLAN Pi archive, with RF calculators and cheat sheets still worth keeping.
resources.description:
wifinigel, by Nigel Bowden (CWNE #135), is an archive of Wi-Fi scripting, WLAN Pi material, Mist API work, RF calculators, and cheat sheets. The last blog post landed in June 2020, so treat the blog itself as an archive. Bowden's WLAN Pi and GitHub tooling carried on well past that, and remains a cornerstone of the field-engineer toolkit.

The cheat sheets and RF calculators are the parts you will keep coming back to, since the math does not age. Read the blog as a reference, and follow Bowden's tooling for the current work. Free, independent. Best for engineers getting into scripting and WLAN Pi.

resources.title: CWNP Certifications and Training
resources.summary: The vendor-neutral enterprise Wi-Fi certification body, CWNA through CWNE, plus a free blog, webinars, and forums.
resources.description:
CWNP is the vendor-neutral enterprise Wi-Fi certification program, going back to 1999. The track runs from CWNA at the foundation up through CWSP, CWDP, CWAP, and CWNE at the top, with additional IoT and administrator paths. If you are serious about Wi-Fi as a career, this is the credential ladder that matters, because it tests how Wi-Fi actually works rather than how one vendor's box is configured.

CWNP sells the exams, training, and study products, and a good amount is free: the blog, webinars, podcasts, whitepapers, and community forums. Start free, learn the body of knowledge, and sit the exam when you are ready. Useful at every level.

resources.title: CWNP Blog
resources.summary: CWNP's free vendor-neutral blog on Wi-Fi fundamentals, RF-myth corrections, and industry news.
resources.description:
The CWNP blog publishes free vendor-neutral Wi-Fi fundamentals, RF-myth corrections, and industry news, written by Tom Carpenter and guest CWNEs. The myth-correction posts are the ones to read, since they take the confidently-wrong claims that float around the industry and set them straight against how Wi-Fi really behaves.

The content maps closely to the CWNP body of knowledge, so it doubles as study reinforcement. Free to read, and CWNP sells certs and training. Useful at every level, and a good companion if you are working toward a CWNP credential.

resources.title: Wi-Fi Alliance Beacon Blog
resources.summary: The Wi-Fi Alliance's free blog on standards, certification, and emerging technology, straight from the source.
resources.description:
The Beacon is the Wi-Fi Alliance's blog, and it is the authoritative voice on the Wi-Fi CERTIFIED program. You get explainers on Wi-Fi 7, Wi-Fi 8, 6 GHz, certification, and emerging technology from the organization that actually defines the interoperability tests.

When you want to know what a Wi-Fi generation officially includes, rather than what a vendor's marketing claims, this is where you check. Free to read. Useful at every level, and the place to go for the definitive line on certification and standards.
